Intro1: Cognitive reflection, or the ability to pause and think carefully rather than relying on gut instincts, is a skill that remains important in everyday life. It helps people evaluate information, weigh alternatives, and make thoughtful decisions rather than jumping to conclusions (Frederick, 2005; Toplak, West, & Stanovich, 2014). This skill can weaken over time if we rely too heavily on shortcuts or external aids instead of exercising our own reasoning.
Intro2: Fortunately, cognitive reflection can be strengthened through practice. Activities such as logic puzzles, reasoning exercises, and multiple-choice problems that require careful consideration encourage people to slow down and analyse situations more deeply. Regular engagement with these types of exercises can help maintain mental flexibility, improve judgment, and keep the mind active and alert (Pennycook, Cheyne, Barr, Koehler, & Fugelsang, 2016).
Ref_Frederick: Frederick, S. (2005). Cognitive reflection and decision making. Journal of Economic Perspectives, 19(4), 25–42.
Ref_Toplak: Toplak, M. E., West, R. F., & Stanovich, K. E. (2014). Assessing miserly information processing: An expansion of the cognitive reflection test. Thinking & Reasoning, 20(2), 147–168.
Ref_Pennycook: Pennycook, G., Cheyne, J. A., Barr, N., Koehler, D. J., & Fugelsang, J. A. (2016). Cognitive style and truth discernment. Judgment and Decision Making, 11(2), 109–120.
